title: Geography
intro: Geography as a quizbowl category draws on physical geography, human geography, and the intersection of place with economics and culture. Questions most often ask for a specific place — a country, city, river, mountain, or desert — identified through a cumulative series of clues about physical features, history, demographics, and economic activity. Sovereign nations and their formal constitutional names are the single largest cluster, followed closely by named physical features: mountain ranges and peaks, deserts, rivers, and lakes recur across the canon, often clued through their role in bordering countries, their length or depth superlatives, or the peoples and cities that depend on them.

A second major cluster covers subnational divisions — American states, Canadian provinces, Australian states and territories, and comparable first-level divisions elsewhere — which quizbowl treats as answerlines in their own right, distinct from the countries containing them. Cities form a third recurring group, ranging from national capitals to major ports and metropolitan hubs, usually clued via their site (the river or coast they sit on), their historical role, or a distinguishing landmark.

Beyond named places, the category regularly tests human and economic geography: indigenous and ethnic groups tied to a specific region, agricultural products and mineral resources associated with particular places, and engineering works — canals, dams, and railways — that have reshaped a landscape. A smaller but persistent set of answerlines covers general geographic concepts and processes, such as desertification or aquifer depletion, that are illustrated with examples drawn from multiple places rather than anchored to one.

Islands, seas, and polar regions round out the canon, frequently overlapping with physical geography (volcanic archipelagos, coral reefs, ice shelves) and with political geography (disputed straits, autonomous island territories, Antarctic claims). Many answerlines in this unit have no dedicated encyclopedia article matched by the pipeline, reflecting how often quizbowl geography rewards broad factual knowledge of a place's basic characteristics rather than a single canonical secondary source.

sections.name: Countries and Sovereign States
sections.blurb: Independent nations, their formal constitutional names, and dependent or autonomous territories.

entries.note: Pacific island nation of North and South Islands; fjords, geysers, and a large Maori population.
Island continent nation; Outback interior, Great Barrier Reef, and marsupial-dominated wildlife.
Horn of Africa nation never colonized; Blue Nile source, Great Rift Valley, Lalibela's rock churches.
Western European nation with overseas departments like French Guiana; noted wine regions and varied terrain.
Island city-state at the tip of the Malay Peninsula; major port and financial center.
Largest South American nation, dominated by the Amazon Basin; Portuguese-speaking.
South Asian nation crossed by the Ganges; Himalayan north and Deccan Plateau south.
Northwestern South American nation touching both Pacific and Caribbean; Andes and Amazon terrain.
World's largest island; autonomous Danish territory, mostly covered by an ice sheet.
Central Asian nation dominated by the Karakum Desert; borders the Caspian Sea.
Landlocked Andean nation sharing Lake Titicaca with Peru; seat of government at La Paz.
North Atlantic island nation, volcanically and geothermally active along the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.
Southeast Asian archipelago of over 17,000 islands, including Java, Sumatra, and Borneo.
Large island nation off East Africa, noted for lemurs and baobab trees found nowhere else.
Central/West African nation called "Africa in miniature" for its varied coast-to-Sahel terrain.
Most populous African nation; Niger River delta, Sahel north, and Gulf of Guinea coast.
Southern South American nation; Pampas grasslands, Andes, and Patagonia, capital Buenos Aires.
English-speaking Central American nation on the Caribbean, home to the Belize Barrier Reef.
Himalayan kingdom between India and China, known for measuring Gross National Happiness.
Scandinavian nation on the Jutland Peninsula; includes Greenland and the Faroe Islands.
Central African nation on the Gulf of Guinea, heavily forested and oil-rich.
Common-link answerline for either the Caucasus nation on the Black Sea or the US state.
British territory on a peninsula at Spain's southern tip, overlooking the Strait of Gibraltar.
Mediterranean nation of thousands of islands and a mainland dominated by the Pindus range.
East African nation on the equator; Great Rift Valley, Mount Kenya, and the Maasai Mara.
Mountainous Central Asian nation dominated by the Tian Shan range; former Soviet republic.
Southern African nation with the Namib and Kalahari deserts and the Skeleton Coast.
Himalayan nation home to Mount Everest and Kathmandu, between India and China.
Andean nation sharing Lake Titicaca with Bolivia; coastal desert, Andes, and Amazon regions.
Southeast Asian archipelago of over 7,000 islands including Luzon and Mindanao.
World's largest country by area, spanning Europe and Asia across Siberia to the Pacific.
Island nation off the southeastern coast of mainland China; capital Taipei.
Landlocked Southern African nation dominated by the Kalahari Desert and Okavango Delta.
World's second-largest country by area, spanning the Pacific to the Atlantic across ten provinces.
Andean nation straddling the equator; includes the Galapagos Islands.
Central European nation on the Danube and Great Hungarian Plain; capital Budapest.
Middle Eastern nation on the Iranian Plateau, bordering the Persian Gulf and Caspian Sea.
Northwest African nation mostly covered by the Sahara Desert.
Landlocked Central Asian nation of steppes and the Gobi Desert, between Russia and China.
Northwest African nation with the Atlas Mountains and Sahara; capital Rabat.
Westernmost European nation on the Iberian Peninsula, facing the Atlantic.
Formal name for India, crossed by the Ganges with Himalayan north and Deccan south.
Formal name for Peru, the Andean nation sharing Titicaca with Bolivia.
Southernmost African nation with three capitals; includes the Cape of Good Hope.
Teardrop-shaped island nation off the southern tip of India.
East African nation home to Mount Kilimanjaro, the Serengeti, and Zanzibar.
Balkan nation on the Adriatic, across from Italy.
Caucasus nation on the Caspian Sea; capital Baku, rich in oil.
Balkan nation on the Black Sea, south of Romania.
Small East African nation on Lake Tanganyika, densely populated.
East Asian nation crossed by the Yangtze and Yellow rivers; world's most populous country.
Formal name for Australia, the island continent nation.
Balkan nation on the Adriatic Sea with the Dalmatian Coast and Plitvice Lakes.
Central African nation drained by the Congo River, formerly Zaire; vast rainforest.
Formal name for Nepal, the Himalayan nation home to Mount Everest.
South American nation on the Atlantic coast, English-speaking, with a vast rainforest interior.
Landlocked Southeast Asian nation along the Mekong River.
Small Mediterranean island nation south of Sicily.
Indian Ocean island nation east of Madagascar, former home of the extinct dodo.
Formal name for Bangladesh, the low-lying delta nation on the Bay of Bengal.
Formal name for China, crossed by the Yangtze and Yellow rivers.
Formal name for Bolivia, the landlocked Andean nation sharing Titicaca with Peru.
Formal name for Panama, the Central American nation home to the Panama Canal.
Central European nation split from Czechoslovakia, with the Tatra Mountains.
Formal name for Oman, the Arabian Peninsula nation on the Gulf of Oman.
Scandinavian nation on the Baltic Sea, largest in area among the Nordic countries.
Landlocked Alpine nation known for neutrality and the Matterhorn.
Southeast Asian nation on the Gulf of Thailand and Andaman Sea; capital Bangkok, never colonized.
Nation spanning Anatolia and a sliver of Europe, split by the Bosphorus at Istanbul.
Central Asian nation, one of only two doubly landlocked countries in the world.
Tiny European microstate in the Pyrenees between France and Spain.
Southern African nation on the Atlantic coast, former Portuguese colony rich in oil and diamonds.
Easternmost Caribbean island nation, a former British colony.
Low Countries nation split between the Flemish north and Walloon south, capital Brussels.
Landlocked Central African nation, one of the world's poorest despite mineral wealth.
Extremely long, narrow South American nation between the Andes and the Pacific.
Small Horn of Africa nation on the Bab-el-Mandeb strait, a strategic Red Sea location.
Mountainous, volcanic Caribbean island nation, not to be confused with the Dominican Republic.
Pacific island nation of hundreds of islands, formerly a US trust territory.
Formal name for Brazil, the largest South American nation.
South Pacific island nation, a melting pot of Melanesian and Indian populations.
Nordic nation of thousands of lakes, bordering Russia and the Baltic Sea.
French-speaking nation on the western third of Hispaniola, poorest in the Western Hemisphere.
Formal name for Iran, the Middle Eastern nation on the Iranian Plateau.
Caribbean island nation south of Cuba, a former British colony known for its Blue Mountains.
East Asian island nation of Honshu, Hokkaido, Kyushu, and Shikoku.
Largest landlocked country in the world, spanning the Central Asian steppe.
Southeastern African nation along the Great Rift Valley, bordering Lake Malawi.
Landlocked West African nation crossed by the Niger River and the Sahara.
Small Eastern European nation between Romania and Ukraine, known for wine.
Southeast Asian nation formerly called Burma, drained by the Irrawaddy River.
Landlocked West African nation, most of it covered by the Sahara.
East Asian nation on the northern Korean Peninsula, capital Pyongyang.
Central American nation home to the Panama Canal linking the Atlantic and Pacific.
Nation occupying the eastern half of New Guinea, extremely linguistically diverse.
Landlocked South American nation on the Parana and Paraguay rivers.
Caribbean island territory of the United States, capital San Juan.
Formal name for Cameroon, the Central/West African nation dubbed "Africa in miniature."
Formal name for Equatorial Guinea, a small Central African nation on the Gulf of Guinea.
Formal name for Honduras, the Central American nation on the Caribbean and Pacific.
Formal name for Kazakhstan, the largest landlocked country in the world.
Formal name for Namibia, the Southern African nation with the Namib and Kalahari deserts.
Central African nation on the Congo River's west bank, capital Brazzaville.
Formal name for Turkey, spanning Anatolia and a sliver of Europe.
Balkan/Eastern European nation on the Black Sea, home to Transylvania and the Carpathians.
Densely populated East African nation of a thousand hills, near Lake Kivu.
Largest Arabian Peninsula nation, home to Mecca and Medina.
Westernmost mainland African nation, surrounding the Gambia.
Indian Ocean island nation northeast of Madagascar.
West African nation on the Atlantic, known for diamond mining and a civil war legacy.
East Asian nation on the southern Korean Peninsula, capital Seoul.
Caribbean island nation of hundreds of islands southeast of Florida.
Southernmost Caribbean island, paired with Tobago, near Venezuela.
North African nation on the Mediterranean, easternmost of the Maghreb.
Tiny Pacific island nation threatened by rising sea levels.
Eastern European nation on the Black Sea, crossed by the Dnieper River.
Southeast Asian nation along the South China Sea, drained by the Mekong and Red rivers.

sections.name: States, Provinces, and Subnational Regions
sections.blurb: First-level administrative divisions such as US states, Canadian provinces, and Australian states/territories.

entries.note: US state of the Great Salt Lake, Wasatch Range, and Mormon-founded Salt Lake City.
Most populous US state; Central Valley, Sierra Nevada, and a long Pacific coastline.
Largest contiguous US state; Gulf Coast, Rio Grande border, and Permian Basin oil fields.
Southwestern US state home to the Grand Canyon and Sonoran Desert.
Southeastern US peninsula; Everglades wetlands, the Keys, and a hurricane-prone coastline.
Northeasternmost US state; rocky Atlantic coast and Acadia National Park.
Southwestern US state along the Rio Grande, with high desert and the Sangre de Cristo mountains.
Northeastern Australian state fronting the Great Barrier Reef; includes Brisbane.
Westernmost Canadian province; Pacific coastline and Rocky Mountains, capital Victoria.
Northern Rocky Mountain US state; Glacier National Park along the Continental Divide.
Arid US state in the Great Basin; home to Las Vegas and part of Lake Tahoe.
Island state south of mainland Australia; wilderness terrain and the Tasmanian devil.
Largest US state by area; Arctic and subarctic terrain, includes Denali and the Aleutians.
Gulf Coast US state; Mississippi River delta, bayous, and the city of New Orleans.
Midwestern US state on Lake Erie, part of the Rust Belt manufacturing region.
Australia's largest state by area, capital Perth, dominated by Outback and mining.
Midwestern US state on Lakes Michigan and Superior, noted for dairy farming.
Southwestern peninsula of England; historic tin mining and Celtic heritage.
Pacific Northwest US state; Willamette Valley, Cascade Range, and Portland.
Mid-Atlantic US state; Appalachian terrain, home to Philadelphia and Pittsburgh.
Region split between Finland and Russia, historically contested and heavily forested.
US state split into two peninsulas, surrounded by four of the Great Lakes.
Northern Great Plains US state with Badlands terrain and the oil-rich Bakken formation.
Canada's largest and northernmost territory, home to a mostly Inuit population.
Great Plains US state, home to the Black Hills and Mount Rushmore.
Least populous US state, home to most of Yellowstone National Park.
Largest Mexican state by area, in the northern desert bordering the US.
Rocky Mountain US state with the highest mean elevation; Denver and the Front Range.
Small New England US state on Long Island Sound, between New York and Massachusetts.
Pacific island US state formed by volcanic hotspot activity; includes Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.
Mountain US state known for the Snake River Plain and potato farming.
Midwestern US state on Lake Michigan, part of the Corn Belt.
Upper Midwest US state of thousands of lakes, on Lake Superior's western shore.
Mid-Atlantic US state between New York City and Philadelphia, on the Jersey Shore.
Great Plains US state; former Indian Territory, Dust Bowl history, and the Wichita Mountains.
Fertile region split between India and Pakistan, watered by five rivers.
High-plateau autonomous region of China, source of the Yangtze, Yellow, Mekong, and Brahmaputra rivers.
Western Canadian province with the Rocky Mountains and oil-sands industry, capital Edmonton.
Historically contested French region on the Rhine, bordering Germany.
South-central US state of the Ozark Mountains and the Mississippi Delta.
Brazilian state on the northeastern Atlantic coast, historic center of the sugar and slave trade.
Central Chinese province on the Yangtze, home to Wuhan and the Three Gorges Dam.
South-central Chinese province south of Hubei, on the Xiang River.
Midwestern US state of rolling farmland in the Corn Belt.
Russian exclave on the Baltic Sea, separated from the rest of Russia by Poland and Lithuania.
Southern US state known for the Bluegrass region and Appalachian coalfields.
Canadian prairie province, capital Winnipeg, with Hudson Bay coastline.
Great Plains US state crossed by the Platte River.
Maritime Canadian province with the highest tides in the world, in the Bay of Fundy.
New England US state with a short Atlantic coastline and the White Mountains.
Southeastern US state from the Appalachians to the Outer Banks.
Southern Mexican state known for its indigenous population and Pacific coastline.
Brazilian coastal state at the easternmost point of South America, capital Recife.
Largest Canadian province by area, predominantly French-speaking, capital Quebec City.
South Indian state on the Bay of Bengal, capital Chennai.
Russian republic in southern Siberia, known for throat singing.
Constituent nation of the United Kingdom, mountainous and Celtic, capital Cardiff.
Pacific Northwest US state on Puget Sound, home to Seattle and Mount Rainier.
Vast autonomous region in northwestern China, home to the Taklamakan Desert and a large Uyghur population.

sections.name: Mountains, Deserts, and Landforms
sections.blurb: Mountain ranges and peaks, deserts, peninsulas, and other terrestrial landforms.

entries.note: Longest continental mountain range on Earth, running the length of western South America.
Desert covering most of Turkmenistan in Central Asia, crossed by the Karakum Canal.
Coastal desert in southwestern Africa running through Namibia, among the oldest deserts on Earth.
Sparsely populated region spanning southern Argentina and Chile, from the Andes to the Atlantic.
Major European mountain range crossing France, Switzerland, Italy, Austria, and Germany.
Volcanic peninsula in Russia's Far East jutting into the Pacific, with many active volcanoes.
Central Asian mountain range spanning Kyrgyzstan, Kazakhstan, and Xinjiang, China.
Large desert spanning northern China and southern Mongolia.
Range traditionally marking the boundary between Europe and Asia within Russia.
Range across Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia, separating the Sahara from the Mediterranean coast.
Ecuadorian volcano whose summit is the point farthest from Earth's center due to equatorial bulge.
Range running along the Adriatic coast through Croatia, Bosnia, and Montenegro.
Second-highest mountain on Earth, on the Pakistan-China border in the Karakoram range.
Dormant Hawaiian volcano; tallest mountain on Earth measured base to peak, site of observatories.
Vast, sparsely populated region of Russia stretching from the Ural Mountains to the Pacific.
Flat-topped table mountains of the Guiana Highlands in Venezuela, home to Angel Falls.
Massive sandstone monolith in central Australia's Outback, sacred to the Anangu people.
Range running along the Iran-Iraq border, source of Tigris and Euphrates tributaries.
Longest continental mountain range on Earth, running the length of western South America.
Driest nonpolar desert on Earth, in northern Chile between the Andes and Pacific.
Range between the Black and Caspian Seas, traditional boundary of Europe and Asia.
Active Ecuadorian volcano in the Andes, one of the highest in the world.
Below-sea-level desert basin in California, hottest and driest place in North America.
Densely populated Central Asian valley split among Uzbekistan, Kyrgyzstan, and Tajikistan.
Steep, glacially carved coastal inlets, most famous in Norway and New Zealand.
Basalt column formation on Northern Ireland's coast, formed by ancient volcanic activity.
Sparsely populated lowland plain spanning Argentina, Paraguay, and Bolivia.
World's highest mountain range, including Everest, separating the Indian subcontinent from the Tibetan Plateau.
Semi-arid sandy region covering much of Botswana, Namibia, and South Africa.
Semi-desert region of South Africa, known for its succulent plant diversity.
Historic mountain pass linking Afghanistan and Pakistan through the Hindu Kush.
Mountainous, lake-filled region of northwest England, inspiration for Romantic poets.
Historic region of northeastern China, industrial heartland bordering Russia and North Korea.
Iconic pyramidal peak on the Swiss-Italian border in the Alps.
Underwater mountain range where tectonic plates diverge, surfacing at Iceland.
Highest peak in the Alps, on the France-Italy border.
Tallest mountain in Africa, a dormant volcano in Tanzania.
Volcano on Martinique whose 1902 eruption destroyed the city of Saint-Pierre.
Range forming the natural border between France and Spain, home to Andorra.
Vast sand desert covering much of the southern Arabian Peninsula, the "Empty Quarter."
Semi-arid transitional belt south of the Sahara, prone to desertification.
Fog-shrouded, shipwreck-strewn Namibian coastline within the Namib Desert.
Large desert in China's Xinjiang region, along the ancient Silk Road.
Highest range of the Carpathians, on the Poland-Slovakia border.
Low-lying, drained marshland region of eastern England.
Historic region of Romania enclosed by the Carpathian Mountains.
Highest peak in Antarctica, part of the Ellsworth Mountains.
Mexican peninsula separating the Gulf of Mexico from the Caribbean, site of Maya ruins.

sections.name: Rivers, Lakes, and Wetlands
sections.blurb: Rivers, lakes, aquifers, and wetlands that shape inland geography.

entries.note: Wide estuary between Argentina and Uruguay formed by the Parana and Uruguay rivers.
River forming much of the US-Mexico border, flowing from Colorado to the Gulf of Mexico.
Central Asian lake drastically shrunk by Soviet-era irrigation diversion of its feeder rivers.
Deepest and oldest freshwater lake on Earth, located in Siberia, Russia.
Central America's largest lake, in Nicaragua, notable for its freshwater bull sharks.
Highest navigable lake in the world, on the Peru-Bolivia border in the Andes.
Africa's largest lake by area, bordered by Uganda, Kenya, and Tanzania; source of the White Nile.
Second-longest river in Africa by length, flowing through the dense Congo Basin rainforest.
Southeast Asian river flowing through China, Laos, Thailand, Cambodia, and Vietnam to its delta.
Largest estuary in the United States, bordered by Maryland and Virginia.
Russian river flowing into the Sea of Azov, historically a Cossack heartland.
River flowing from Tibet through Pakistan, cradle of the ancient Indus Valley Civilization.
Large lake/inlet in Venezuela known for oil deposits and frequent lightning storms.
Largest of the Great Lakes by surface area, bordering the US and Canada.
Longest river in Europe, flowing through Russia into the Caspian Sea.
Longest river in Asia, flowing across China and site of the Three Gorges Dam.
China's second-longest river, called the "cradle of Chinese civilization"; prone to flooding.
Largest river by discharge volume, flowing across South America through the Amazon rainforest.
Europe's second-longest river, flowing from Germany through Vienna and Budapest to the Black Sea.
Hypersaline lake between Israel and Jordan; lowest point on Earth's land surface.
East African Rift lake bordered by Malawi, Mozambique, and Tanzania; also called Lake Nyasa.
Large shallow lake in central Florida, source of the Everglades' water flow.
Wide basin of the Congo River between Kinshasa and Brazzaville.
Vast underground water source beneath the US Great Plains, critical for irrigation.
Longest river in South Africa, forming part of the border with Namibia.
Major river of Venezuela and Colombia, flowing into the Atlantic through a large delta.
World's largest tropical wetland, mostly in Brazil, rich in wildlife.
Shallow saline lake in Southern California's desert, formed by an early-1900s canal breach.
Large estuary on the California coast, spanned by the Golden Gate and Bay bridges.
Central Asian river flowing from the Pamirs toward the Aral Sea, historically called the Oxus.
Underground water-bearing rock layers, like the Ogallala, critical for irrigation and drinking water.
Underwater sinkholes found in the Bahamas and Belize, popular with divers.
Major South Asian river flowing from Tibet through India and Bangladesh.
River that carved the Grand Canyon, flowing from the Rockies toward the Gulf of California.
Europe's second-longest river, flowing from Germany through Vienna and Budapest to the Black Sea.
West African river that gives its name to the narrow nation of the Gambia.
Largest lake entirely within Canada, in the Northwest Territories near the Arctic Circle.
Wetland straddling Virginia and North Carolina, historically a refuge for escaped enslaved people.
Principal river of Myanmar, flowing south into the Andaman Sea.
Largest lake in Central Europe, a popular resort area in Hungary.
Shallow Sahel lake bordering Chad, Nigeria, Niger, and Cameroon, dramatically shrinking.
East African Rift lake between Rwanda and the DRC, notable for dissolved methane gas.
Large alpine lake on the California-Nevada border in the Sierra Nevada.
Second-deepest lake in the world, bordering Tanzania, DRC, Burundi, and Zambia.
Largest lake in Turkey, a soda lake in the country's east.
General term for inland standing bodies of water, a common category answerline.
One of the longest rivers in the world, flowing through Siberia to the Arctic Ocean.
Colombia's principal river, flowing north into the Caribbean.
Major West African river, giving its name to both Niger and Nigeria.
African river that ends in an inland delta in Botswana rather than reaching the sea.
Italy's longest river, flowing across the northern plain to the Adriatic.
Deep inlet of the Pacific in Washington state, surrounded by Seattle and Tacoma.
Balkan river flowing through Slovenia, Croatia, and Bosnia into the Danube.
Large estuary on Florida's Gulf Coast, surrounded by Tampa and St. Petersburg.
River flowing through Tennessee and Alabama, dammed extensively by the TVA.

sections.name: Oceans, Seas, and Islands
sections.blurb: Seas, gulfs, straits, and the islands and archipelagos found within them.

entries.note: World's second-largest island, split between Indonesian Papua and independent Papua New Guinea.
World's largest inland body of water, bordered by Russia, Iran, and three Central Asian states.
Spanish volcanic archipelago off northwest Africa, including Tenerife and Mount Teide.
French Pacific island territory near Australia; nickel-rich with an extensive coral lagoon.
Northernmost main island of Japan; cold and volcanic, home to the city of Sapporo.
Second-largest Mediterranean island, an Italian region known for nuraghi and rugged terrain.
Archipelago at South America's southern tip, split between Argentina and Chile.
Largest Mediterranean island, an Italian region off the toe of the Italian boot, near Mount Etna.
Distinctively K-shaped Indonesian island east of Borneo.
Third-largest island in the world, divided among Indonesia, Malaysia, and Brunei.
US territory island in the western Pacific, part of the Mariana Islands.
Southernmost Chinese province, a tropical island in the South China Sea.
Island extending east from New York City, including the Hamptons, Brooklyn, and Queens.
Sea between Great Britain and continental Europe, rich in oil and gas deposits.
Contested sea bordered by China and Southeast Asian nations, containing the Spratly Islands.
South Pacific island nation, an archipelago of volcanic islands east of Australia.
Sea separating the Italian Peninsula from the Balkans.
Swedish-speaking autonomous island region of Finland in the Baltic Sea.
Indonesian island known for Hindu temples and terraced rice paddies.
Massachusetts peninsula enclosing Cape Cod Bay, known for beaches and cranberry bogs.
Remote Chilean Pacific island famed for its moai stone statues.
Ecuadorian volcanic archipelago in the Pacific famed for finches that inspired Darwin.
Self-governing British Crown dependency in the Irish Sea.
Largest and most populous island of the Philippines, home to Manila.
Portuguese volcanic archipelago in the Atlantic, known for its fortified wine.
Narrow sea between Africa and Arabia, connected to the Mediterranean via the Suez Canal.
Small sea between the Aegean and Black Seas, bisected by Istanbul.
Remote Yemeni island in the Indian Ocean known for otherworldly dragon's blood trees.
Larger, more mountainous of New Zealand's two main islands, home to the Southern Alps.
Norwegian Arctic archipelago, home to the Global Seed Vault.
British Caribbean territory southeast of the Bahamas, known for coral reefs.
Largest Pacific island off North America's west coast, part of British Columbia.
Second-largest ocean, separating the Americas from Europe and Africa.
Northern European sea bordered by Scandinavia, the Baltic states, Poland, and Germany.
Strait at the southern tip of South America, disputed between Argentina and Chile.
Narrow strait separating Russia and Alaska, linking the Pacific and Arctic Oceans.
Sea bordered by Turkey, Russia, and Ukraine, connected to the Mediterranean via the Bosphorus.
Strait separating New Zealand's North and South Islands.
Largest Greek island, in the eastern Mediterranean, home to ancient Minoan civilization.
Autonomous Danish archipelago in the North Atlantic between Iceland and Norway.
World's largest coral reef system, off the coast of Queensland, Australia.
Sea between Mexico's Baja Peninsula and its mainland, also called the Sea of Cortez.
Atlantic bight off West Africa, bordering Nigeria, Ghana, and other coastal states.
Atlantic basin bordered by the US Gulf Coast, Mexico, and Cuba.
Third-largest ocean, bordered by Africa, Asia, and Australia.
Strait between Vancouver Island and Washington state, also names a Pacific tectonic plate.
Southernmost of Japan's four main islands, volcanically active.
Remote volcanic island off eastern Australia, part of New South Wales.
Middle Eastern sea bordered by Iran and Arabian Peninsula states, rich in oil.
Remote British Pacific territory settled by Bounty mutineers.
Russian island in the Pacific north of Japan, at times disputed with Japan.
Ocean surrounding Antarctica, sometimes considered the fifth ocean.
Contested South China Sea archipelago claimed by China and several Southeast Asian nations.
Strait separating Australia's Cape York Peninsula from Papua New Guinea.
Semi-autonomous archipelago off Tanzania, historic center of the spice and slave trade.

sections.name: Cities and Urban Geography
sections.blurb: Major cities and metropolitan areas around the world.

entries.note: South African coastal city below Table Mountain, near the Cape of Good Hope.
Former British colony, now a Chinese special administrative region on the Pearl River Delta.
Capital of Thailand on the Chao Phraya River; known for canals and ornate temples.
Capital of Argentina on the Rio de la Plata estuary; European-style architecture and tango.
Capital of the Democratic Republic of the Congo, on the Congo River across from Brazzaville.
Administrative capital of Bolivia; world's highest capital city, set in the Andean altiplano.
Former capital and largest city of Nigeria; major West African port on the Gulf of Guinea.
Second-largest city in Canada, on an island in the Saint Lawrence River in Quebec.
Capital of Azerbaijan on the Caspian Sea, a below-sea-level city built on oil wealth.
Capital of Denmark on the island of Zealand, linked to Sweden by the Oresund Bridge.
China's largest city, a major port on the Yangtze River delta.
Capital of Ethiopia and seat of the African Union, in the country's highlands.
Major US city on Lake Michigan, Illinois; noted for its skyline and rail hub.
Capital of Bangladesh, on the Buriganga River in the Ganges Delta.
Wealthy city in the United Arab Emirates known for skyscrapers and artificial islands.
Second-largest US city, in Southern California, sprawling along the Pacific coast.
Brazilian city deep in the Amazon rainforest, once a rubber-boom capital.
Capital of Kenya, a major East African hub near Nairobi National Park.
Capital of Western Australia, one of the world's most isolated major cities.
Capital of the Czech Republic on the Vltava River.
Largest city in Brazil and the Southern Hemisphere, a major financial center.
Pacific Northwest US city on Puget Sound, near Mount Rainier.
Capital of Turkmenistan, known for extensive white-marble architecture.
Capital of Kazakhstan, formerly renamed Nur-Sultan, a planned city on the steppe.
Capital of Egypt on the Nile, near the Giza pyramids; largest city in Africa.
Largest city on New Zealand's South Island, rebuilt after a major 2011 earthquake.
Turkish city straddling the Bosphorus, split between Europe and Asia.
Former ultra-dense, largely ungoverned enclave in Hong Kong, demolished in the 1990s.
Capital of Malaysia, home to the Petronas Towers.
Nevada desert city built around casino tourism, near Lake Mead.
Former Portuguese colony, now a Chinese special administrative region known for casinos.
Capital of the Philippines, on Manila Bay on the island of Luzon.
Capital of Mexico, built on the drained lakebed of the former Aztec capital Tenochtitlan.
Louisiana city on the Mississippi River delta, below sea level and levee-protected.
Largest US city, at the mouth of the Hudson River on a fine natural harbor.
Capital of North Korea, on the Taedong River.
Italian city built on a lagoon of the Adriatic, famed for its canals.
Capital of South Australia, on the Gulf St Vincent.
Port city on the Chesapeake Bay's Patapsco River estuary.
English city famed for Roman-era hot springs, in Somerset.
Capital of Germany, on the Spree River, divided by a wall during the Cold War.
Capital of Colombia, a high-altitude city on the Andean plateau.
Capital of Hungary, formed by uniting Buda and Pest on the Danube.
Major US city on Lake Michigan, noted for its skyline and rail hub.
Michigan city on the Detroit River across from Canada, historic auto-manufacturing center.
Capital of Scotland, built around a volcanic crag topped by its castle.
Italian Renaissance city on the Arno River in Tuscany.
Major port city in southern China on the Pearl River, near Hong Kong.
Northeastern Chinese city known for Russian architectural influence and its ice sculpture festival.
Largest city in Texas, a major oil and space-industry hub on Buffalo Bayou.
City at the confluence of the Kansas and Missouri rivers, known for jazz and barbecue.
Capital of Nepal, in a Himalayan valley.
Capital of Portugal, on the Tagus River estuary near the Atlantic.
Capital of the United Kingdom, on the River Thames.
Capital of Angola, a major Atlantic port city.
Third-largest city in Sweden, connected to Copenhagen by the Oresund Bridge.
Colombia's second-largest city, in a mountain valley of the Andes.
South Florida city on Biscayne Bay, a major Latin American financial gateway.
Northern Italian city in Lombardy, a fashion and financial capital.
Capital of Tennessee, on the Cumberland River, known as country music's home.
Capital of Tennessee, on the Cumberland River, known as country music's home.
Pacific Northwest city at the confluence of the Willamette and Columbia rivers.
California city on a peninsula between the Pacific and San Francisco Bay.
Spanish city in Galicia, endpoint of the Camino pilgrimage route.
Largest city on Japan's Hokkaido island, known for its snow festival.
Capital of Bosnia and Herzegovina, site of the assassination that sparked World War I.
Pacific Northwest US city on Puget Sound, near Mount Rainier.
Capital of South Korea, on the Han River.
Southern Spanish city in Andalusia, on the Guadalquivir River.
Missouri city at the confluence of the Mississippi and Missouri rivers, marked by the Gateway Arch.
Capital of Japan, the world's most populous metropolitan area, on Tokyo Bay.
Capital of Mongolia, one of the coldest capital cities in the world.
Spanish Mediterranean city known for its City of Arts and Sciences and paella.
Pacific coastal city in British Columbia, Canada, near the US border.
Pacific coastal city in British Columbia, Canada, near the US border.
Capital of Austria on the Danube, historic Habsburg imperial seat.
Russian Pacific port city, terminus of the Trans-Siberian Railway.
Capital of the United States, on the Potomac River, not part of any state.
Official capital of Ivory Coast, home to a giant basilica.

sections.name: Peoples and Cultural Geography
sections.blurb: Ethnic groups, indigenous peoples, and distinctive human-geographic phenomena.

entries.note: Ethnic enclaves of Chinese immigrant communities found in cities worldwide, from San Francisco to Manila.
Informal urban settlements characteristic of Brazilian cities, especially Rio de Janeiro.
Indigenous Polynesian people of New Zealand.
Indigenous people of the Andean altiplano in Bolivia and Peru.
Adjective for the Netherlands, its people and language; tied to polders and dikes reclaiming land from the sea.
Indigenous Arctic people of Canada, Greenland, and Alaska.
Gambling establishments defining places like Las Vegas, Macau, and Monaco.
Burial grounds notable as land-use features, from Pere Lachaise to Arlington.
Singular form of the burial-ground answerline, referencing a specific notable graveyard.
Afro-Indigenous people of Central America's Caribbean coast, descended from shipwrecked Africans and Caribs.
Stateless ethnic group spanning Turkey, Iraq, Iran, and Syria in the region of Kurdistan.
Pastoralist ethnic group of Kenya and Tanzania near the Serengeti and Maasai Mara.
Human- or cycle-powered transport common in South and Southeast Asian cities.
Indigenous reindeer-herding people of northern Scandinavia and Russia's Kola Peninsula.
Portable round tents traditionally used by nomadic peoples of Central Asia and Mongolia.

sections.name: Economic Geography: Resources and Agriculture
sections.blurb: Crops, minerals, and other resources tied to specific places and industries.

entries.note: Agricultural product tied to regions like Bordeaux, Napa Valley, and Tuscany; frequent common-link answerline.
Resource tied to places like Bolivia's salt flats and historic trans-Saharan salt-trade routes.
Mineral resource tied to Chile's Atacama mines and Michigan's Upper Peninsula.
Agricultural crop historically tied to the US South and Egypt's Nile Delta.
Fruit crop underlying wine industries in regions like Bordeaux and Napa Valley.
Staple crop grown in flooded paddies across South and East Asia.
Tropical fruit crop tied to Central American "banana republic" plantation economies.
Staple grain crop dominant in the US Midwest's Corn Belt.
Radioactive mineral mined in places like Kazakhstan, Niger, and Australia.

sections.name: Infrastructure and Engineering
sections.blurb: Canals, dams, railways, and other built features that reshape geography.

entries.note: Massive hydroelectric dam on the Parana River, jointly operated by Brazil and Paraguay.
Engineered waterways such as the Panama, Suez, and Erie canals that reshape trade and geography.
Libyan pipeline network moving fossil aquifer water from the Sahara to coastal cities.
Undersea rail tunnel connecting England and France beneath the English Channel.
Japan's high-speed "bullet train" rail network.

sections.name: National Parks and Protected Areas
sections.blurb: National parks, nature reserves, and polar research stations.

entries.note: Southernmost, ice-covered continent with no permanent population, governed by international treaty.
US national park on Maine's rocky Atlantic coast, including Mount Desert Island.
California national park in the Sierra Nevada, famed for granite cliffs like El Capitan.
Largest research station in Antarctica, operated by the United States.
Antarctica's largest ice shelf, in the Ross Sea near McMurdo Station.
US national park mostly in Wyoming, sitting atop a volcanic hotspot with geysers like Old Faithful.
Canada's oldest national park, in the Alberta Rockies.
US national park in Montana's Rocky Mountains, named for its shrinking glaciers.
Norwegian-claimed sector of Antarctica.

sections.name: Geographic Concepts and Processes
sections.blurb: General geographic terms, natural processes, and phenomena not anchored to one place.

entries.note: Pre-Incan archaeological site near Lake Titicaca in Bolivia.
The academic discipline itself, occasionally clued directly as a meta or common-link answerline.
Caribbean coastal region of Nicaragua and Honduras, home to the Miskito people.
Narrow Namibian panhandle extending east to touch Zambia, Zimbabwe, and Botswana.
Slow-moving ice masses found from Patagonia to Greenland, retreating amid climate change.
Ancient Indus Valley Civilization city site in present-day Pakistan.
Continental political and economic union of African states, headquartered in Addis Ababa.
Naturalist whose namesake line divides Asian and Australian fauna through the Indonesian archipelago.
World's tallest uninterrupted waterfall, dropping from a tepui in Venezuela.
Iconic swollen-trunked tree of Madagascar and mainland Africa's savannas.
Color linking geographic features such as the Blue Nile, Blue Ridge Mountains, and Blue Grotto.
Warm, dry wind that descends the eastern Rocky Mountains across the Great Plains.
Waterborne disease famously mapped by John Snow to a London water pump.
Process by which fertile land turns to desert, notably along the Sahel.
Marine mammals found in the Indian and Pacific Oceans, including near the Great Barrier Reef.
Roman town buried by Vesuvius's eruption alongside Pompeii.
Primates found only on Madagascar and nearby islands.
Ancient city carved into rose-colored cliffs in Jordan.
Color linking geographic features such as the Red Sea, Red River, and Red Square.
Arctic and subarctic animal herded by the Sami people of Scandinavia.
De facto autonomous Kurdish-led region in northeastern Syria.
Fish that migrate up rivers like the Columbia to spawn, tied to Pacific Northwest geography.
Prehistoric stone monument on Salisbury Plain in England.
Cascading water features such as Angel Falls, Niagara, and Victoria Falls.
Palestinian territory west of the Jordan River, disputed between Israel and Palestinians.
